## 1. Introduction

Can regime-aware evolutionary optimisation render a structurally negative-expectancy trading strategy *survivable* under realistic execution costs, and what mechanism does the survival depend on? This research formulates that as a binary research question with a follow-on mechanism interrogation:

> **RQ1 (binary):** Does an island-model genetic algorithm whose topology is *derived from* a discovered regime structure bound the loss envelope of a grid-hedged Martingale strategy on EUR-USD under real OANDA execution costs?
> **RQ2 (mechanism):** If so, *which component* of the architecture — selectivity at entry, exposure compression, depth-distribution shift, or some combination — supplies the bound, and is the bound a structural consequence of the architecture or a statistical artefact of the evaluation window?

**Scope commitment.** This dissertation answers RQ1 and RQ2 for **Iteration 1** of the pipeline (20-gene per-regime genome covering sizing, grid, and take-profit parameters; 12,600 production-engine evaluations over 36 months of training data; 15.5-month strictly out-of-sample evaluation). **Iteration 2** (57 genes adding entry-signal, filter, and risk-management evolution) is implementation-complete and architecturally validated under a 3-month pre-flight (§6.8 / Appendix F), and its full-scale 15.5-month OOS evaluation is the principal target of the conference/journal-paper extension (§8.1). Reviewers should read §6 results as Iteration 1 results; Iteration 2 mentions in §3.4.1, §5.6, §6.8, §7.8, and §8.1 are scope-of-future-work pointers, not deferred dissertation claims.

The choice of grid-hedged Martingale on liquid spot FX is deliberate: it is the *most adverse* publicly studied strategy class against which to test such a claim. The class is widely deployed in retail forex, has high per-cycle win rates and rare-but-catastrophic failures, and accumulates spread cost multiplicatively with depth — properties that exhaust naïve fixed-parameter configurations and make the strategy a stringent test of any adaptive risk-management layer rather than a strawman (mathematical structure: §2.4). A method that bounds losses on this class generalises *more easily* to milder strategies than the converse.

Grid-hedged Martingale profitability depends critically on the relationship between grid spacing, multiplier progression, and the volatility regime of the traded instrument. A configuration that performs well in low-volatility, mean-reverting markets can exhaust its depth capacity rapidly during sustained directional trends, resulting in catastrophic drawdown — making fixed parameter configurations structurally inadequate across changing market conditions (Hamilton, 1989; Nystrup et al., 2020). The fundamental challenge is combinatorial: a Martingale strategy with *k* tunable parameters operating across *r* market regimes requires *r* independent parameter configurations, each validated against the specific statistical properties of its regime. Manual calibration is impractical beyond a handful of regimes, and naive grid search scales exponentially with parameter dimensionality (the curse of dimensionality; Bellman, 1957). Evolutionary computation offers a principled approach but standard genetic algorithms evolve a single population whose fitness is averaged across all market conditions; when the fitness landscape differs materially between regimes, this averaging rewards mediocre-everywhere parameters over optimal-in-any-single-regime parameters (Goldberg & Richardson, 1987; Eiben & Smith, 2015).

Prior approaches to parameter adaptation fall in three categories. *Online optimisation* applies gradient-based or bandit methods continuously (Li et al., 2010; Agrawal & Goyal, 2013) but assumes smooth parameter-performance surfaces, struggling with the discontinuous transitions characteristic of regime change. *Regime detection plus parameter lookup* switches between pre-calibrated sets via a regime classifier (Nystrup et al., 2017; Palupi et al., 2021), but requires both the regime set and optimal parameters to be specified a priori, limiting discovery of unseen market states. *Global evolutionary search* (Aguilar-Rivera et al., 2015; Chideme, Chen, & Lin, 2025) evolves a single solution that compromises across all market conditions.

This research proposes a synthesis of these approaches through the island-model genetic algorithm framework (Whitley et al., 1999; Alba & Tomassini, 2002). Market regimes define natural boundaries for evolutionary isolation: parameters that perform well in one regime may be detrimental in another, and maintaining distinct populations per regime prevents the averaging effect that degrades global optimisation (Wright, 1931; Whitley et al., 1999).

A critical requirement in evolutionary trading system design is that fitness evaluation must use the same execution model as deployment. Many evolutionary trading studies evaluate on simplified simulators that omit spread, slippage, margin constraints, and execution latency, then deploy on real brokers — a simulation-to-production gap analogous to the sim-to-real transfer problem in robotics (Tobin et al., 2017). The gap is acute for Martingale-family strategies because spread cost accumulates multiplicatively with depth (each hedge level adds one spread charge), so the simulator-to-production discrepancy compounds with depth. This research closes the gap by training island evolution directly on the production backtesting engine with the full OANDA CFD execution model; each genome's fitness evaluation runs a complete backtest through the same engine used for live deployment. The methodological cost is substantial — see §4 for per-evaluation budget and §7.4 for the empirical evidence that simplified-simulator genomes do not transfer — and the ethical framing of this choice is developed in §7.10.

The proposed architecture, IslandPilot, operates as a strategy-agnostic pipeline that wraps any trading strategy without modifying its source code. It contributes the following:

1. A hierarchical two-level Gaussian Mixture Model for regime discovery, where macro-level clustering captures broad market states and sub-level clustering within each macro-state captures finer structural distinctions, with BIC-based model selection at both levels.

2. An island-model genetic algorithm where each regime leaf maintains an isolated population that evolves execution parameters independently, with sibling migration enabling genetic exchange between sub-regimes within the same macro-cluster.

3. A hysteresis-based regime inference mechanism that prevents classification whipsaw by requiring a confidence margin before switching regimes, with a grace period that suppresses entry signals during regime transitions.

4. A multi-factor adaptive position sizing layer that scales trade size according to regime classification confidence, evolved base parameters, and current drawdown state, enforcing survivability constraints.

5. Empirical validation on EUR-USD 5-minute data (full OANDA CFD execution engine with real per-candle bid-ask spread, swap, and slippage costs) on a 15.5-month strictly out-of-sample window (January 2025 to mid-April 2026; trained exclusively on 2022–2024). The headline result — drawdown collapsed by approximately two orders of magnitude, profit factor lifted while remaining sub-unity — is reported in Table 5 (§6.1). The pipeline does not manufacture directional alpha absent from the underlying random-entry signal; it collapses the drawdown envelope through regime-conditioned depth capping and session selectivity.

The remainder of this research is organised as follows. Section 2 reviews related work in regime detection, evolutionary optimisation for trading, and island-model genetic algorithms. Section 3 describes the system architecture in detail, including formal algorithm specifications. Section 4 presents the training methodology. Section 5 describes the experimental setup. Section 6 reports results including per-regime breakdowns. Section 7 discusses the findings with extended analysis. Section 8 concludes and identifies directions for future work.
